items.q: Why did Britain win the Battle of Britain?
items.correct: Radar, the Dowding command system, home advantage in pilot recovery, German tactical errors (switching to bombing cities), and adequate fighter production.
items.misconception: The RAF won because Spitfires were better than any German aircraft.
items.random_wrong: Hitler called off the attack to invade the USSR immediately.
items.why: The Bf 109 was comparable to the Spitfire. Victory came from integrated air defence and German strategic mistakes, not aircraft superiority — a key point in modern historiography.

items.q: What was the Elizabethan Religious Settlement of 1559?
items.correct: A deliberate via media — a Protestant doctrine and English-language prayer book combined with some Catholic-style ceremony — designed to maximise conformity.
items.misconception: Elizabeth made England fully Protestant and banned Catholic worship outright.
items.random_wrong: Elizabeth reunited England with the Pope to make peace with Spain.
items.why: The settlement was a compromise designed to be broadly acceptable. Students assume binary Protestant/Catholic divides; Elizabeth deliberately blurred them to keep her realm stable.

items.q: What were the Wars of the Roses really about?
items.correct: Dynastic conflict between rival branches of the Plantagenets (Lancaster and York) over the throne, intensified by weak kingship under Henry VI and noble factionalism.
items.misconception: The whole country was at war for thirty continuous years.
items.random_wrong: They were a religious war between Catholics and Protestants.
items.why: Fighting was sporadic and most of England saw no battles; ordinary life carried on. Students imagine total civil war; in reality, it was an aristocratic dispute fought in short campaigns.
The answer examiners want is: Walsingham. “Cecil” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Where was Mary Queen of Scots executed?
items.why: It's Fotheringhay. “Tower of London” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was Mary Queen of Scots executed?
items.why: Exact answer: 1587. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
